svg.title: Five-question design-review checklist for drug-discovery ML projects
svg.desc: A two-column reference card with five numbered rows. Each row pairs a design question on the left with the failure mode it prevents on the right. The five questions are: does the molecular representation match the task; is the activity-prediction loss calibrated to the assay's noise; does the train and test split respect chemical-series correlation; is the docking pose physically plausible before the score is trusted; and does the program treat ADMET as a multi-objective constraint rather than a single filter. A header summarises the move; a footer points to the relevant chapter sections.
svg.text: DRUG-DISCOVERY ML DESIGN REVIEW — FIVE QUESTIONS, FIVE FAILURE MODES
Run before the experiment; re-run before trusting another paper's headline number.

g.text: Does the molecular representation match the task?
Fingerprint for fast similarity / property baselines; GNN for SAR with held-out scaffolds;
3-D pharmacophore or pose for binding-mode questions; SMILES-LM for generation.
Wrong representation — features cannot express the signal
2-D fingerprint on a stereochemistry-driven task; pure SMILES-LM on a 3-D pocket-fit question.
Is the loss matched to the assay's noise and dynamic range?
Predict log(IC₅₀) or pIC₅₀, not IC₅₀. Use Huber or quantile loss when censoring is present.
Treat right-censored "> 10 μM" as a one-sided constraint, not a single number.
Loss mis-specification — assay artefacts dominate
MSE on raw IC₅₀ with five orders of magnitude; ignored qualifiers; unweighted PAINS in training set.
Does the train / test split respect chemical-series correlation?
Scaffold split (Bemis — Murcko) at minimum; time split for prospective realism;
cluster split (Butina / Tanimoto < 0.4) for honest generalisation across series.
Series leakage — test pairs are analogues of training compounds
Random 80/20 on ChEMBL bioactivities; cluster splits done at the assay rather than at the compound.
Is the docking pose physically plausible before the score is trusted?
Inspect contacts; check clashes; confirm key interactions; watch for buried polar groups;
use re-docking RMSD on a known co-crystal as a calibration baseline for the system.
Score without pose check — ranking artefacts dominate
High-ΔG poses with buried unsatisfied polars; DiffDock outputs unconstrained by re-docking RMSD.
Is ADMET treated as a multi-objective constraint rather than a single filter?
Track Pareto-optimal compounds across potency, hERG, microsomal stability, solubility, permeability.
Use uncertainty-aware predictors; add active learning to choose the next batch.
Single-axis optimisation — wins potency, fails the clinic
Picking the most potent analogue every round; ignoring hERG / hepatotoxicity until pre-clinical.

svg.text: Most "AI for drug discovery" controversies trace to one of these five questions; run the checklist before reading the headline.
The companion checklist for genomics ML in Chapter 16 has the same shape — the failure modes generalise.
